skill.name: get-available-resources
skill.description: Detects local compute resources for scientific workloads, including CPU cores, GPUs, memory, disk space, operating system details, and recommended processing strategies.
skill.summary: Detect local compute resources before running large scientific workloads.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged dynamic imports, subprocess usage, filesystem writes, weak-crypto patterns, and system reconnaissance. Review found no malicious intent, network exfiltration, environment secret access, or prompt injection, but the skill intentionally inventories local hardware and runs hardcoded system utilities. Publish with a warning that it creates a local resource report containing system details.

medium_findings.title: Local System Reconnaissance
medium_findings.description: The script intentionally collects operating system, CPU, memory, disk, and GPU details to guide scientific workload decisions. This is core functionality, but the generated report may disclose sensitive host inventory if shared outside the project.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly gathers host inventory with psutil and platform APIs. The behavior is intentional and documented, but it can reveal local system details.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded Local Hardware Probe Commands
medium_findings.description: The script runs nvidia-smi, rocm-smi, sysctl, and system_profiler with fixed arguments to detect GPU hardware. The commands are not built from user input, so command injection evidence was not found.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each subprocess call uses a static argument list and a timeout. The pattern is real local command execution, but the semantic context supports legitimate device discovery.

low_findings.title: User-Selected Output Path Writes Resource Report
low_findings.description: The CLI accepts an output path and writes the resource report there. This is expected behavior, but users should avoid pointing it at important files because existing content can be overwritten.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The write operation and CLI output argument are explicit. The impact is limited because the file content is a local JSON report and no privilege escalation path is evident.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Finding Dismissed
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography findings at the skill description and argparse description do not correspond to hash, cipher, or random-number code. No evidence found for weak cryptographic algorithm usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain natural-language descriptions, not cryptographic operations. Nearby code does not import or call cryptographic libraries.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Import Used for Timestamp
dangerous_patterns.description: The __import__ call dynamically imports datetime only to create an ISO timestamp. This is a true pattern match but a false positive for dynamic code loading risk because the module name is a hardcoded standard-library string.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The imported module name is constant and not user-controlled. There is no evidence of loading attacker-controlled modules or executing arbitrary code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess Hardware Probes
dangerous_patterns.description: The subprocess.run findings are true positives for local command execution, but the calls use fixed argument arrays for GPU and Apple Silicon detection. No shell=True use or user-controlled command construction was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly executes local binaries. The fixed arguments and short timeouts reduce injection risk, but the behavior remains relevant for marketplace disclosure.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Write
dangerous_patterns.description: The file write finding is a true positive because the script writes a JSON resource report. The path is controlled by the CLI argument, and no path restriction is enforced.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write call is direct and easy to verify. The practical risk is low because it writes structured local output for the declared purpose.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation Backtick Matches
dangerous_patterns.description: The SKILL.md external-command matches are fenced JSON, Python, and shell examples or inline command references, not hidden execution logic. They document running the script, installing psutil, checking permissions, and reading the generated report.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is documentation text and code examples. The actual executable behavior is in scripts/detect_resources.py and is covered separately.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation System-Reconnaissance Match
dangerous_patterns.description: The system-reconnaissance static match in SKILL.md appears on a recommendation line about sequential processing for small core counts. The actual host inventory behavior is real and evaluated separately in the script findings.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is explanatory documentation, not executable reconnaissance code. The confidence is slightly lower because the skill as a whole does perform resource inventory elsewhere.

dangerous_patterns.title: Prompt Injection Check
dangerous_patterns.description: No evidence found for override instructions, fake system messages, pre-approval claims, or requests to skip security analysis in the reviewed files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill header and body describe resource detection without instructing the evaluator to ignore external instructions. No prompt injection phrases were observed.

content.value_statement: Large scientific tasks can fail when available compute resources are unknown. This skill checks local CPU, GPU, memory, disk, and OS details before recommending practical compute strategies.
actual_capabilities: Detects physical and logical CPU core counts with processor architecture.
Reports total, available, used, and swap memory through psutil.
Checks disk capacity and available space for the working path.
Detects NVIDIA, AMD, and Apple Silicon GPU backends when local tools are available.
Writes a local resource report with strategy recommendations for parallelism, memory use, GPU acceleration, and large data handling.
Supports a verbose mode that prints the collected report to the terminal.
limitations: Requires psutil to be installed before the script can run.
GPU detection depends on local utilities such as nvidia-smi, rocm-smi, sysctl, or system_profiler.
Resource readings are point-in-time snapshots and can change during a workload.
The generated report may contain host inventory details and should be shared carefully.

use_cases.title: Plan a Large Data Analysis
use_cases.description: Check memory and disk space before choosing pandas, Dask, Zarr, H5py, or Parquet.

use_cases.title: Prepare Model Training
use_cases.description: Detect available GPU backends before selecting PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX, CuPy, or CPU-only execution.

use_cases.title: Size Parallel Workloads
use_cases.description: Use CPU core counts and memory availability to choose a safe worker count before batch processing.

prompt_templates.title: Check Resources First
prompt_templates.prompt: Use get-available-resources before starting this analysis. Summarize the available CPU, memory, disk, and GPU resources in plain language.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner resource check before a new scientific task.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Data Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Run get-available-resources, then recommend whether this dataset should use in-memory loading, chunking, Dask, Zarr, H5py, or Parquet.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate planning for a large dataset.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Model Acceleration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use get-available-resources to detect local GPU support. Recommend the best training backend and a fallback when no GPU is available.
prompt_templates.scenario: Model training setup with hardware-aware choices.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Compute Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Run get-available-resources and use the report to design a full execution plan for parallel workers, memory handling, GPU use, and intermediate files.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning for a complex scientific workflow.

output_examples.input: Check this workstation before processing a 50 GB genomics dataset.
output_examples.output: The machine has enough CPU parallelism, but memory is limited for direct loading. Use chunked processing with Dask or Zarr and keep intermediate files compressed.

output_examples.input: Find out whether I can train this neural network with GPU acceleration.
output_examples.output: A compatible GPU backend was detected. Use the matching deep learning backend, and keep a CPU fallback for environments without the same hardware.

output_examples.input: Decide how many workers to use for processing 10,000 files.
output_examples.output: Use a worker count below the total logical cores and monitor memory pressure. Reduce workers if each task loads large input files.

best_practices: Run the skill before expensive analysis, model training, or large file processing.
Keep the report local when it includes machine inventory or project paths.
Re-run detection when moving to another machine or after resource-heavy programs start.
anti_patterns: Do not assume GPU libraries are available only because a GPU is present.
Do not share the generated resource report publicly without reviewing host details.
Do not use the suggested worker count without considering per-task memory use.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates a local resource report with CPU, GPU, memory, disk, operating system, and compute strategy details.

faq.question: Does it send data over the network?
faq.answer: No network behavior was found in the reviewed files. The script writes a local report.

faq.question: Why does it run system commands?
faq.answer: It uses local hardware utilities to detect NVIDIA, AMD, and Apple Silicon GPU capabilities.

faq.question: Can I choose the output file?
faq.answer: Yes. The CLI supports a custom output path, so choose a path that will not overwrite important files.

faq.question: What dependency is required?
faq.answer: The script requires psutil. Other imported modules are from the Python standard library.

faq.question: Is the resource report always accurate?
faq.answer: No. It is a snapshot of current system state, and available memory or disk space can change quickly.
